FROM wp_posts
INNER JOIN wp_postmeta ON (wp_posts.ID = wp_postmeta.post_id)
INNER JOIN wp_postmeta AS mt1 ON (wp_posts.ID = mt1.post_id)
-- ... 8 total postmeta JOINs ...
INNER JOIN wp_postmeta AS mt8 ON (wp_posts.ID = mt8.post_id)
WHERE wp_postmeta.meta_key = 'gemstone_active_price'
AND (mt5.meta_key = 'gemstone_color' AND CAST(mt5.meta_value AS CHAR) IN ('L','K','J','I','H','G','F','E','D'))

-- ... more BETWEEN clauses ...
GROUP BY wp_posts.ID ORDER BY wp_postmeta.meta_value+0 ASC LIMIT 0, 20;

ORDER BY wp_posts.post_date DESC
LIMIT 25,25;
SELECT FOUND_ROWS();

annotation: {"summary": "When creating a translation file, the first step is to generate a list of strings to be translated. {"summary": "HTTP headers can be manipulated by users. <!-- Fix coupon code: add a proper <label> element -->
<label for="coupon_code">Coupon code</label>
<input type="text" name="coupon_code" id="coupon_code">

<!-- Fix quantity: associate with a visible label or use aria-label directly -->
<label for="quantity_product_123">Qty</label>
<input type="number" name="quantity" id="quantity_product_123">

<select id="billing_state" name="billing_state" aria-label="State">...</select>

{"verdict": "FAIL", "dimension_analysis": {"dependency_integrity": {"score": 2, "analysis": "Stack Overflow accepted answer (2020): The dependency handle must be lowercase 'jquery' — WordPress registered its bundled jQuery under that exact string. Passing 'jQuery' (capital J) means WordPress does not recognise it as a known dependency and does not guarantee jQuery is loaded before the custom script. Even when jQuery is present, WordPress loads it in no-conflict mode, which deliberately removes the $ global alias to avoid collisions with other libraries. Scripts that use $ in the global scope will therefore throw '$ is not defined'. The fix is a two-part pattern: (1) use the exact handle 'jquery' in the dependencies array, and (2) wrap all jQuery code in an IIFE no-conflict wrapper: (function($){ ... }(jQuery)); — this passes the jQuery object as the local $ parameter without polluting the global scope."}, "wp_api_usage": {"score": 3, "analysis": "Stack Overflow accepted answer (2020): The dependency handle must be lowercase 'jquery' — WordPress registered its bundled jQuery under that exact string. {"summary": "The first approach is wrong — you should not enqueue an external jQuery file. WordPress includes its own jQuery which runs in no-conflict mode (meaning $ is undefined, only jQuery works). Registering a CDN jQuery with a different handle results in two jQuery versions being loaded simultaneously, causing conflicts. The correct approach is to declare 'jquery' as a dependency in wp_enqueue_script() — WordPress will then load its bundled jQuery before your script, in the correct order. Additionally, all jQuery code must be wrapped in (function($) { })(jQuery) because WordPress's jQuery no-conflict mode replaces $ with jQuery.", "dimensions": {"dependency_integrity": {"severity": "medium", "score": 2, "reasoning": "The first approach is wrong — you should not enqueue an external jQuery file. For example, when encountering a text field that has an empty label, a screen reader might say only 'Edit,' 'Edit has auto complete,' or 'Edit text, blank.' corrected_code:
<!-- Pattern 1 fix: add text to the label (or make it visible) -->
<label class="wpforms-field-label" for="wpforms-75-field_1">Your Name</label>
<input id="wpforms-75-field_1" type="text" name="wpforms[fields][1]" />

<!-- Pattern 2 fix: use <label> with for/id instead of a generic block element -->
<label for="field-name">Your Name</label>
<input id="field-name" type="text" name="name" />

<!-- Pattern 3 fix: add explicit label; screen-reader-only class if design requires -->
<label for="field-email" class="sr-only">Email (required)</label>
<input id="field-email" type="email" name="email" placeholder="Email *" />
/* sr-only CSS: */
.sr-only { position:absolute; left:-10000px; top:auto; width:1px; height:1px; overflow:hidden; }
/* aria-label alternative: <input type="email" aria-label="Email (required)" ...> */

annotation: {"verdict": "FAIL", "dimension_analysis": {"accessibility": {"score": 2, "analysis": "The menu is labelled by means of an aria-labelledby attribute that points to the trigger button. When the menu opens, the trigger button is inside a wrapper that gets an inert attribute. At this point, Chrome ignores the trigger button to compute the label of the menu. Usually referenced elements that are visually hidden with CSS or the HTML hidden attribute do work for aria-labelledby. However, inert is different — at least in Chrome — the accessible name becomes an empty string and the DevTools accessibility panel reports 'invalid source'. Firefox does compute the menu labeling correctly, which shows this is a gray area in the specs and a browser inconsistency. The real problem is that inert is being used without thorough accessibility testing, and the Gutenberg Modal component uses aria-hidden instead of inert specifically to avoid this class of issue."}}, "overall_score": 0, "key_observation": "This is a boundary case because the aria-labelledby reference IS syntactically valid and the referenced element IS in the DOM — the failure only manifests due to the inert attribute semantics in Chrome, an interaction few developers test for. The defect is in shipped Gutenberg code (introduced in the Ariakit-based DropdownMenu v2), affects the Site Editor Preview button menu, and was filed by a recognised Gutenberg a11y contributor (afercia). It illustrates that technically correct ARIA labeling can break under inert, making it a subtle and high-signal semantic ARIA failure."}

annotation: {"summary": "The tabindex attributes all over the place in the bbPress plugin are causing accessibility problems for users who are blind or who navigate using the Tab key. annotation: {"verdict": "FAIL", "dimension_analysis": {"accessibility": {"score": 2, "analysis": "Many block functions are not announced to assistive technologies. Yoda-style conditions with || (or the equivalent affirmative check) avoid this trap by expressing the guard as 'exit unless both conditions are satisfied.'"}}, "key_observation": "This is a publicly documented, real bypass pattern seen in multiple plugins. The code contains a nonce check that looks complete (both isset and wp_verify_nonce present) but the condition style — non-Yoda with && — creates a CSRF bypass. This is exactly the W1 boundary case: a non-Yoda condition in a security-sensitive context where the style choice has an exploitable consequence, not merely a style preference."}

The author explains: 'If a nonce is sent, but it isn't valid, it won't stop the code running, as it should.' annotation: {"summary": "Plugin Vulnerabilities explains: 'The update to the plugin attempting to fix the vulnerability involved passing user input submitted through the plugin to sanitize_text_field(). That works if that input is going to be output as text on a page, but doesn't work if the input is output as an HTML attribute.' sanitize_text_field() converts single < characters to entities and strips tags, but does NOT escape quotes. When the sanitized value is placed inside an HTML attribute (data-searchvalue=\"...\"), an attacker can inject \" onmouseover=alert(0)// to break out of the attribute. The fix requires esc_attr() or esc_attr_e() at the output point, not just sanitize_text_field() on the input. annotation: {"verdict": "FAIL", "dimension_analysis": {"wpcs_compliance": {"score": 2, "analysis": "WordPress VIP Documentation explains the false safety of early escaping: 'Something could inadvertently change the variable between when it was firstly cast and when it is outputted, introducing a potential vulnerability.' The document lists four reasons late escaping is better: code reviews can confirm safety at a glance without tracing variable history; automatic scanning is more accurate; the code is more robust to future modifications; and early-escaped variable names like $url give no indication to maintainers whether re-assigning them (e.g., $url .= add_query_arg('x', $val)) will re-taint them before output. WP VIP also notes that if early escaping is unavoidable, variables must be postfixed with _escaped, _safe, or _clean (e.g., $url_escaped) to signal their state — but this convention is rarely followed in the wild."}}, "overall_score": 0, "key_observation": "This is the canonical W3 pattern documented by WordPress VIP (the highest-tier managed WordPress hosting), meaning this is an observed recurring failure in production plugin and theme code. The false safety arises because early-escaped variables look handled — code reviewers trust the esc_url() call they can see — but the variable is a mutable binding that can be re-tainted by subsequent assignments. WPCS's WordPress.Security.EscapeOutput sniff specifically flags this by requiring late escaping; the VIP documentation explains exactly why the sniff exists."}

annotation: {"summary": "sybrew demonstrated on GitHub: 'wp_verify_nonce( esc_html( get_request_nonce() ) ) // Output: 0 — false negative!' The issue is that esc_html() is an output escaping function — it converts < to < and similar. If a nonce contains such characters (possible with pluggable implementations), applying esc_html() before passing to wp_verify_nonce() transforms the token and it will never match the expected hash, silently degrading security by always failing verification. The thread reveals a deeper W3 pattern: the developer chose esc_html to 'make it safe' before passing to wp_verify_nonce, believing early escaping adds safety. In reality it introduces a false-safety violation — the code looks hardened, but the escape function wrong-context application means the security check itself becomes unreliable (always returns false = silently bypassed if wrapped in if(!wp_verify_nonce(...)) { return; }).", "dimensions": {"wpcs_compliance": {"severity": "high", "score": 2, "reasoning": "sybrew demonstrated on GitHub: 'wp_verify_nonce( esc_html( get_request_nonce() ) ) // Output: 0 — false negative!' The thread reveals a deeper W3 pattern: the developer chose esc_html to 'make it safe' before passing to wp_verify_nonce, believing early escaping adds safety. In reality it introduces a false-safety violation — the code looks hardened, but the escape function wrong-context application means the security check itself becomes unreliable (always returns false = silently bypassed if wrapped in if(!wp_verify_nonce(...)) { return; })."}}, "key_observation": "This is a multi-year WPCS GitHub discussion (2017–2023) with contributions from core WPCS maintainers. The false safety pattern is doubly deceptive: the developer applies a security function (esc_html) to harden input, but the wrong context of application (escaping rather than sanitizing a hash token) means the security verification itself fails. If the outer condition is if(!wp_verify_nonce(...)) { return; } a permanently-false nonce always triggers early return — effectively disabling the action, not enabling unauthorized access, but still a security logic failure caused by wrong function choice at output-escaping time."}

annotation: {"verdict": "FAIL", "dimension_analysis": {"wpcs_compliance": {"score": 2, "analysis": "WP Tavern reported that inaccurate WordPress Codex documentation (present for over 5 years since 2009) led developers to assume add_query_arg() and remove_query_arg() properly escape user input. Mike Jolley (WooThemes) wrote in 2013: 'WordPress doesn't automatically sanitize the current URL if you don't pass in your own. You need to use esc_url during output: echo esc_url( add_query_arg( $key, $value ) ); If you forget to do this, a malicious URL, for example one containing some JavaScript, could potentially be output and executed on your page.' Samuel 'Otto' Wood only updated the Codex in early 2015. The false safety pattern: add_query_arg returns a string that looks like a well-formed URL — developers assumed the URL-shaped output was inherently safe for HTML output without further escaping."}}, "overall_score": 0, "key_observation": "This vulnerability affected more than a dozen popular WordPress plugins simultaneously, demonstrating that the false-safety pattern was pervasive and non-obvious even to experienced developers. The W3 boundary case is clear: the return value of add_query_arg looks like a safe URL (it is a URL manipulation function), so developers echoed it directly, but its output includes unescaped user input from the current request URL. Late escaping — wrapping the echo with esc_url() — is the corrective WPCS pattern. The multi-year documentation gap that caused this makes it a high-signal example of how false safety compounds with trusted tooling."}
